lines.text: APPENDIX
Key dates and milestones.
1670s: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek observes "animalcules" — bacteria — under his homemade microscope.
1880s: Robert Koch establishes pure-culture microbiology and the postulates that define causal microbial agents.
1977: Carl Woese and George Fox publish the discovery of Archaea as a third domain of life, using 16S rRNA comparative sequencing.
1985: Norman Pace proposes culture-independent 16S sequencing of environmental samples; foundational papers follow through 1990.
1990: SAR11 clade identified by 16S sequencing of Sargasso Sea samples; remains uncultured for over a decade.
1998: First bacterial genome (Haemophilus influenzae) had been sequenced (1995); microbial genomics enters maturation.
2002: First cultured representative of SAR11 (Pelagibacter ubique) isolated.
2006: Turnbaugh et al. publish microbiome-obesity association in mouse model.
2007: Human Microbiome Project (HMP) launched by NIH.
MetaHIT launched in Europe.
2009-2013: Tara Oceans expedition collects global ocean microbiome samples.
2010: Earth Microbiome Project founded.
2012: HMP phase one main results published.
American Gut Project launched.
2013: van Nood et al. publish randomized trial of FMT for recurrent C. diff in NEJM; stopped early for efficacy.
2014: crAssphage identified by cross-assembly of gut metagenomes.
2015: Tara Oceans flagship papers published in Science; teixobactin discovered.
2016: DADA2 published; HMP phase two (iHMP) ongoing through 2016.
2016: Sender, Fuchs, and Milo revise bacterial-to-human cell ratio estimate to closer to 1:1.
2022: Rebyota (Ferring) approved by FDA; first defined microbiome-derived product for C. diff.
2023: Vowst (Seres Therapeutics) approved by FDA; oral capsule formulation.
Key people.
Antonie van Leeuwenhoek: discoverer of microbes.
Robert Koch: father of pure-culture microbiology.
Carl Woese: discoverer of Archaea; pioneer of 16S phylogenetics.
Norman Pace: pioneer of culture-independent microbial ecology.
Sallie Chisholm: discoverer of Prochlorococcus; pioneer of marine microbial ecology.
Jeff Gordon: pioneer of gut microbiome research; mentored many leaders of the field.
Rob Knight: co-founder of Earth Microbiome Project and American Gut; major figure in microbiome bioinformatics.
Peer Bork: bioinformatics leadership of MetaHIT, Tara Oceans, and many other large consortia.
Shinichi Sunagawa: bioinformatics of Tara Oceans.
Chris Bowler, Eric Karsenti: scientific leadership of Tara Oceans.
Eugene Koonin: computational discovery of new CRISPR systems and microbial gene families.
Jill Banfield: pioneer of metagenome-assembled genomes and uncultured-organism genomics; co-discoverer of CasX.
Tanja Woyke: leadership in single-cell microbial genomics at JGI.
Jennifer Doudna, Emmanuelle Charpentier, Feng Zhang: CRISPR-Cas9 development.
Ben Callahan, Susan Holmes: DADA2 development.
Christina Warinner: ancient oral microbiome research.
Svante Pääbo: ancient DNA pioneer (Neanderthal genome).
Justin Sonnenburg, Erica Sonnenburg: gut microbiome physiology and diet interventions.
Martin Blaser: "missing microbes" hypothesis; antibiotic effects on microbiome.
Kim Lewis: iChip, teixobactin discovery.
Robert Edwards: crAssphage discovery; computational viromics.
Key tools and pipelines.
16S analysis: QIIME2, DADA2, Mothur.
Reference databases: SILVA, GreenGenes, RDP.
Shotgun metagenomics: Kraken2, MetaPhlAn, HUMAnN (read-based); MEGAHIT, metaSPAdes (assembly); MetaBAT2, CONCOCT, MaxBin2 (binning); CheckM (MAG quality assessment).
Viromics: VirSorter, DeepVirFinder, VIBRANT.
Biosynthetic cluster mining: antiSMASH.
Single-cell amplification: MDA with phi29 polymerase.
Things worth reading or watching.
Pace, N.
R.
"A molecular view of microbial diversity and the biosphere.
" Science (1997).
Turnbaugh et al.
"The Human Microbiome Project.
" Nature (2007).
Sender, Fuchs, Milo.
"Revised estimates for the number of human and bacteria cells in the body.
" PLoS Biology (2016).
The 2015 Tara Oceans flagship papers in Science.
van Nood et al.
"Duodenal infusion of donor feces for recurrent Clostridium difficile.
Callahan et al.
"DADA2: High-resolution sample inference from Illumina amplicon data.
" Nature Methods (2016).
A note on the limits of this lecture.
This script has covered the methodological backbone of the microbiome field and the main areas of application.
It has not gone deep on every body site, every disease association, every analytical method, or every commercial player.
The field is moving fast; specific results and players may have changed by the time you watch or read this.

The underlying methods — culture-free DNA sequencing, multi-omics, metagenome assembly and binning, single-cell genomics, ancient DNA — are durable, and the conceptual frame they enable will outlast any specific result.
Build your understanding around the methods and the conceptual frame; treat any specific clinical claim as provisional, especially the ones that get the most press attention.
